summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the problem of careless email communication, noting that the ease of sending emails often leads to thoughtless messages. He shares guidelines from Ole Eichhorn and 43 Folders about writing effective emails, including avoiding criticism via email, being judicious about recipients, and keeping messages concise. The post extends these principles to all communication methods, arguing that easier communication requires more deliberate thought. Atwood concludes that the real skill lies in knowing when to escalate between communication channels.
key_points: The ease of sending email inversely correlates with the effort people put into composing them
Email criticism and technical debates should be handled face-to-face or by phone instead
Effective emails require clear goals, strong subject lines, and brevity that fits on one screen
The same communication principles apply across all channels - IM, phone, and face-to-face
Choosing the right communication medium for the situation is itself a crucial skill
money_quotes: It's easy to fire off an email with barely any effort at all. And that's exactly how much effort goes into most emails: none.
Remember that email is a public and permanent record.
Assume no one will read more than the first two sentences of your email.
The key difference is the amount of effort required for each communication method: the easier it is, the more you need to consider before doing it.
The real art is knowing when to escalate from IM to email, from email to face-to-face, and when to drop the ultimate communication A-bomb: calling a meeting.
key_insight: The easier a communication method is to use, the more deliberate thought you should put into whether and how to use it.
